coredata.dc:description: In this study, bio-based composite films with enhanced barrier, mechanical, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties were prepared by incorporating Stropharia rugosoannulata waste (SP)-derived polysaccharides (SPP) and carbon dots (SPCDs) as an active substance into gelatin (GEL) matrix, which were evaluated for their potential application in pork preservation. The effects of varying concentrations of SPCDs on the morphology, microstructure, and overall performance of the films were systematically investigated. The findings demonstrated that the addition of SPCDs to the GEL/SPP matrix significantly improved the mechanical strength, water vapor permeability, and ultraviolet light blocking ability of the composite films. Specially, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films containing 3 % carbon dots exhibited radical scavenging rates of 53.74 % for DPPH and 73.19 % for ABTS, along with inhibition rates of 58.37 % for E. coli and 70.25 % for S. aureus. Preservation experiments revealed that the prepared GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % films provided superior preservation effect on pork, highlighting their potential as eco-friendly active food packaging materials.

head.ce:title: Characterization of gelatin-based composite films loaded with polysaccharide and carbon dots from Stropharia rugosoannulata and their application in pork fresh-keeping

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Food Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Food Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Food Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650500, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, bio-based composite films with enhanced barrier, mechanical, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties were prepared by incorporating Stropharia rugosoannulata waste (SP)-derived polysaccharides (SPP) and carbon dots (SPCDs) as an active substance into gelatin (GEL) matrix, which were evaluated for their potential application in pork preservation. The effects of varying concentrations of SPCDs on the morphology, microstructure, and overall performance of the films were systematically investigated. The findings demonstrated that the addition of SPCDs to the GEL/SPP matrix significantly improved the mechanical strength, water vapor permeability, and ultraviolet light blocking ability of the composite films. Specially, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films containing 3 % carbon dots exhibited radical scavenging rates of 53.74 % for DPPH and 73.19 % for ABTS, along with inhibition rates of 58.37 % for E. coli and 70.25 % for S. aureus. Preservation experiments revealed that the prepared GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % films provided superior preservation effect on pork, highlighting their potential as eco-friendly active food packaging materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-biodegradable packaging materials derived from petroleum can take centuries to decompose, resulting in serious environmental pollution and health concerns [1–3]. This has driven scientists to develop sustainable and renewable alternatives. The increasing consumer preference for fresh, safe, and superior quality food products underscores the urgent need to innovate bio-based, compostable, and sustainable packaging options [4,5]. This need is particularly critical for meat products, which are prone to lipid oxidation and microbial contamination during processing, storage, and distribution [6,7]. The spoilage of meat leads to considerable financial losses, underscoring the necessity for packaging materials with strong antimicrobial and antioxidant properties.

Gelatin (GEL), a type of animal protein obtained from by-products of animal slaughter and processing [8], has gained popularity in food packaging due to its remarkable capabilities for forming films, alongside its edibility, biodegradability, abundance, and cost-effectiveness [9]. Despite these advantages, pure gelatin films exhibit several limitations, such as poor mechanical strength, low barrier properties, and high moisture sensitivity, which hinder their effectiveness in food packaging applications. To overcome these shortcomings, various natural substances and active compounds, including polysaccharides, polyphenols, and nanoparticles, have been incorporated into the gelatin matrix to create composite films with enhanced performance [10].

Polysaccharides, composed of >10 monosaccharide residues linked by glycosidic bonds, play critical roles in various biological processes, including cell recognition, growth, differentiation, metabolism, embryonic development, carcinogenesis [11], viral infection [12], and immune response [13]. These functions make polysaccharides a key area of research in modern medicine and food science. Stropharia rugosoannulata is rich in essential amino acids, polysaccharides, and minerals, which contribute to its health benefits, such as reducing blood cholesterol, purifying blood, softening blood vessels, and preventing coronary heart disease, hyperlipidemia, arteriosclerosis, and alleviating neurasthenia [14]. Polysaccharides extracted from Stropharia rugosoannulata exhibit excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, water solubility (WS), film-forming properties, and strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activities, making them ideal active additives for films [15,16]. Stropharia rugosoannulata Polysaccharides (SPP) can also form hydrogen bonding with gelatin to improve the mechanical and barrier properties of composite films. Given the substantial production of Stropharia rugosoannulata in Yunnan, significant amounts of waste are generated during harvesting, providing an abundant resource for our experiments.

Incorporating carbon dots into composite films is an effective strategy to enhance the performance of packaging materials. Carbon dots, which are <10 nm in size, represent a novel class of carbon nanomaterials known for their antioxidant, antibacterial, non-toxic, biocompatible, and cost-effective properties, as well as their UV shielding capabilities and ease of synthesis [17,18]. These carbon dots can be derived from various food processing by-products and natural resources, with Stropharia rugosoannulata being an excellent source due to its rich active substances and high carbohydrate content. By utilizing the residual material from Stropharia rugosoannulata after polysaccharide extraction, we efficiently create carbon dots, demonstrating a sustainable approach to waste resource utilization.

In this study, we developed an active packaging film with multifunctional capabilities for pork preservation by using GEL as the matrix and incorporating SPP and carbon dots (SPCDs) derived from Stropharia rugosoannulata waste as functional additives. The morphology and physicochemical characteristic of composite films were thoroughly investigated. Additionally, the mechanical, optical, thermal, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of the prepared GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films were systematically evaluated. These multilayer composite films were then applied to the packaging of fresh pork stored at 4 °C, with the physicochemical and microbiological properties of the pork assessed over a 10-day period. The extraction of bioactive compounds from Stropharia rugosoannulata waste and their incorporation into functional food packaging materials can significantly enhance the shelf life of food products while simultaneously reducing the disposal of Stropharia rugosoannulata waste and facilitating its high-value utilization.

GEL, sourced from bovine bone, was acquired from Aladdin Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycerol was procured from Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Stropharia rugosoannulata was obtained from a local market in Kunming, China. Chemicals including 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), 2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), terephthalic acid (TPA), 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), and CCK8 were supplied by Aladdin Co., Ltd., Tianjin, China. All other reagents, of analytical grade, were utilized as received without further purification.

SPP was extracted using a hot water leaching method [19]. 2 g of ground Stropharia rugosoannulata powder was dissolved in 50 mL of distilled water and heated in a water bath at 75 °C for 2 h. The solution was then filtered, and the filtrate was precipitated with 75 % ethanol. Free proteins in the resulting precipitate were removed using the Sevag method (chloroform: n-butanol = 4:1). After a second ethanol precipitation, the SPP was freeze-dried and stored for subsequent use.

The weight-average molecular weight (Mw), monosaccharide composition, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) determination methods of SPP were detailly described in the supplementary materials.

The antioxidant capacity of SPP at varying concentrations was assessed through DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays. In the DPPH test, 0.4 mL of various concentrations of SPP was combined with 2 mL of a 0.1 mM solution of DPPH in ethanol and left to react in the dark for 30 min. A UV–Vis spectrophotometer was then utilized to measure the absorbance at 517 nm. For the ABTS assay, an ABTS solution of 7.4 mM mixed with 2.6 mM potassium persulfate was incubated in darkness for 16 h to generate radicals. Afterwards, 0.5 mL of SPP at differing concentrations was blended with 4 mL of this ABTS solution and incubated at 30 °C for 6 min. The absorbance was measured at 734 nm. The radical scavenging activity was determined utilizing the formula [20]:
Radical scavenging rate
%
=
A 0
−
A f
A 0
× 100 %
where A0 and Af are the absorbance values of DPPH solution and ABTS solution in the absence and presence of samples, respectively.

Standard curves were established by measuring the clearance of DPPH and ABTS by ascorbic acid solutions at different concentrations. The antioxidation capacity (AC) was expressed as the equivalent mass [μg] of ascorbic acid per [mg] of SPP [21,22].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of SPP and SEM images of bacteria treated with SPP
ce:section.ce:para:
The plate counting method was employed to assess the antibacterial activity of SPP composite films against Escherichia coli (E. coli, ATCC25922) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, ATCC6538). Briefly, 0.2 mL of different concentrations of SPP solution were sterilized using ultraviolet light for a duration of 20 min and then combined with 5 mL of bacterial solution (106 CFU/mL). The mixture was then incubated at 37 °C and shaken at 120 rpm for 6 h. Subsequently, 0.5 mL from this incubation mixture was taken, mixed into 4.5 mL of sterile saline, and three serial dilutions were performed. A 0.1 mL sample from the final dilution was then plated onto LB agar and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The number of colonies that developed was counted to assess bacterial growth. A bacterial suspension without SPP served as the control. The rate of bacterial growth inhibition was calculated using the following equation [23,24]:
Bacteriostatic Rate
%
=
N 0
−
N i
N 0
× 100 %
where N0 and Ni are the number of the colonies of the control group and the tested samples, respectively.

And then the visible colonies treated with 1.5 mg/mL SPP were carefully transferred to a centrifuge tube using a spatula, followed by the addition of PBS. The samples were then subjected to centrifugation at 2000 revolutions per minute (rpm) for 10 min. Post-centrifugation, the supernatant was removed, and a 2.5 % glutaraldehyde fixative, pre-cooled to 4 °C, was gently added along the inner wall of the tube. The samples were subsequently stored at 4 °C overnight. Each sample underwent a dehydration process using a graded series of ethanol solutions (30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 80 %, 95 %, and 100 %), with each concentration applied for 10 min. The samples were then dried using a critical point dryer, coated with gold nanoparticles (Au NPs), and the surface morphology was examined using a scanning electron microscope (SU8010 Hitachi, Japan).

SPCDs were synthesized utilizing a single-step hydrothermal process following the method reported by Li et al. [25]. Briefly, the aqueous solution residue after the polysaccharide extraction of Stropharia rugosoannulata was filtered, and 2 g of the residue was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. This mixture was then transferred into a stainless-steel reactor with a polytetrafluoroethylene lining (Teflon). The reactor was then heated to 200 °C and maintained at this temperature for 6 h using a silicone oil bath. Following the reaction, the reactants were centrifuged for 20 min at 10,000 rpm as the temperature dropped to 25 °C. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and lyophilized to yield a brownish-black SPCD powder.

The structure of SPCDs was assessed using various analytical techniques. A transmission electron microscope (FE-TEM, Talos F200S, FEI, USA) provided detailed images of the SPCDs' morphology. Fluorescence spectrometry (F-4600, Hitachi, Germany) analyzed the optical properties of the SPCD solutions, which were further studied across a spectral range of 200–800 nm using UV–Vis spectrophotometry. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, captured using an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan), spanned a 2θ range from 5° to 85°, revealing the crystalline structure of the SPCDs. Lastly, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet Is5, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) was utilized to identify the chemical groups within the SPCDs, scanning from 4000 to 500 cm−1.
The antioxidant properties of different concentrations of SPCDs were evaluated using DPPH and ABTS free radical scavenging assays using the methods described above.

The inhibition zone method was used to assess the antibacterial activity of SPCDs against E. coli and S. aureus. A 0.1 mL aliquot of a bacterial suspension (109 CFU/mL) was spread onto LB agar plates, and a well was created in the medium using a sterilized punch. Into each well, 0.1 mL of a 100 mg/mL SPCD solution was injected. The plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, after which the size of the inhibition zone was measured.

Composite films of GEL/SPP/SPCDs were produced using the traditional solvent casting technique. A 2 wt% GEL solution was prepared by dissolving GEL powder in deionized water, followed by heating at 50 °C with constant stirring. SPP powder (1 wt% based on GEL) was then dissolved in GEL solution. A plasticizer, consisting of 20 wt% glycerol (calculated based on GEL) was added, and the mixture was stirred continuously for 1 h to create the GEL/SPP solution. Subsequently, various amounts of SPCDs (0, 1, 3, 5 and 7 wt%, relative to GEL) were incorporated and mixed vigorously for 20 min to ensure even distribution within the GEL/SPP solution. The mixtures were then sonicated for 15 min to remove air bubbles. Next, 100 g of the mixture was cast onto a square Teflon plate with a side length of 20 cm and allowed to dry in a fume hood at room temperature. After drying, the films were carefully removed from the plates and placed in a sealed desiccator at 50 % RH and 25 °C for 48 h to equilibrate the water content [25]. Based on the SPCD content (0, 0.2 mg/mL, 0.6 mg/mL, 1 mg/mL, and 1.4 mg/mL, respectively), the prepared films were referred as GEL/SPP, GEL/SPP/SPCDs-1 %, GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 %, GEL/SPP/SPCDs-5 %, and GEL/SPP/SPCDs-7 %, respectively. The specific flowchart of composite film preparation was shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), XRD and FTIR analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The surface morphology of the composite films was observed using a scanning electron microscope (MIRA LMS, Tescan, Czech Republic) after coating each sample with gold. The chemical groups on the surface of the composite films were analyzed using an FTIR spectrometer equipped with an ATR attachment, scanning within the wavelength range of 4000–500 cm−1. The crystalline structure of the composite films was examined using an XRD over a 2θ range of 5° to 85° at a voltage of 40 kV. Finally, normalization was performed for XRD and FTIR results. The percent relative degree of crystallinity (Xc) was calculated utilizing the following equation [26]:
X c
=
A c
A c
+
A a
where Ac and Aa represent the areas of the crystalline and amorphous regions in the XRD pattern, respectively.

The mechanical properties of the films, including tensile strength (TS), modulus of elasticity (EM), and elongation at break (EB), were evaluated utilizing a tensile testing machine (Qunlong Instruments Co., Ltd., Xiamen, China). The thickness of the films was measured at 10 randomly selected points on each set of films using a portable digital micrometer. Rectangular strips of the films, measuring 1.5 cm by 8 cm, were clamped into the machine with an initial gap length of 50 mm and stretched to the breaking point at a tensile speed of 50 mm/min under a tensile force of 200 N. Each set of films underwent at least five parallel tests. TS (MPa), EB (%), and EM (MPa) were calculated utilizing the following equations [27]:
TS
MPa
=
F
W × T
EB
%
=
∆ L
L
× 100
EM
MPa
=
σ ε
where F signifies the maximum tensile force (N), W represents the film width (mm), T is the film thickness (mm), ΔL indicates the length change at the breaking point (mm), and L is the initial length of the film (mm). σ is the stress (MPa) and ε is the strain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water vapor permeability (WVP) and water resistance
ce:section.ce:para:
The WVP of the films was determined gravimetrically according to ASTM E96-92 [28]. The test film sample was tightly sealed over the mouth of the weighting bottle filled with anhydrous pre-dried color change silica gel. The weighing bottle was then placed in a desiccator set at 75 % RH and 25 °C. The bottle was weighed every 1 h until the weight stabilized. Five parallel experiments were conducted for each group of films. The WVP of the film samples was calculated as follows:
WVP
g · m /
m 2
· Pa · s
=
∆ m × d
∆ t × A × ∆ P
where Δm/Δt (g/s) is the weight gain per unit time, ΔP (Pa) is the water vapor pressure across the film at 25 °C, d (m) is the film thickness, and A (m2) is the area available for permeation.

Water solubility (WS) and water contact angle (WCA) are used to determine the water resistance of the film. 0.5 g of the film sample was dried at 105 °C for 24 h and the initial dry film mass was measured. The film samples were then immersed in 50 mL of distilled water and left at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the film mixture was filtered and dried at 105 °C for 24 h to obtain an insoluble dry film. The WS of the film sample was calculated as follows:
WS
%
=
m 0
− m
m 0
× 100 %
where m0 (g) was the weight of the initial dry films; and m (g) was the weight of the insoluble dry films.

An optical contact angle tester (JY-PHa, Chengde Jinhe Instrument Manufacturing Co., Ltd., Chengde, China) was used to measure the water contact angle (WCA) of the film samples. 5 μL of droplet of deionized water was applied to the surface of each film, and the procedure was replicated five times for consistency across all samples [29].

The transmittance of each group of films within the 200–800 nm range was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, with an empty quartz cell serving as the control. The perceived hue of the films was evaluated using a 3NH portable colorimeter (NR 110, 3nh Co. Ltd., Shenzhen, China), with a white standard plate (L* = 99.99, a* = 0.18, b* = −0.86) as the reference. The total color difference (ΔE) was calculated using the following equation:
ΔE =
L ∗
− L
2
+
a ∗
− a
2
+
b ∗
− b
2
where L, a and b represent the color parameters of the film samples. Each film was measured at five different locations.

The thermal characteristics were analyzed with a thermogravimetric analyzer (TG 209 F1, Netzsch, Germany). About 10 mg of each film sample in an alumina crucible was heated to 800 °C with a temperature increase of 20 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere.

The antioxidant properties of the film samples were measured through DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays, as described earlier. However, in this case, 10 mg of film samples were reacted with 5 mL of ABTS and 5 mL of DPPH solutions under dark conditions for 24 h. Antioxidant capability (AC) was expressed as the equivalent mass [μg] of ascorbic acid per 10 mg of film sample.

The plate counting method was applied to determine the growth inhibition of GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films against E. coli and S. aureus. The specific procedures were consistent with the above methods. Experiments were conducted using 10 mg film samples.

The ability of the composite films to generate OH under visible light irradiation was assessed using TPA. For this assay, 0.005 g of the film samples were added to 30 mL of 0.5 mM TPA solution, which was then illuminated using a photoreactor equipped with a white LED light. After filtration, the fluorescence emission intensity of the produced 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTPA) was measured at 430 nm using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Measurements were taken at 10-min intervals over 60 min [30].

The generation of superoxide anions (O2−) by the composite films was evaluated by monitoring the oxidative bleaching of DPBF, a quencher of O2−, under visible light. A 100 μL solution of DPBF in DMF (10 mM) was added to 30 mL of the film sample suspension (0.005 mg/mL), which was then exposed to visible light (LED). Approximately 3 mL of the reaction solution was sampled every minute, and the fluorescence emission intensity was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm [31].

The cytotoxicity of the films was determined by CCK8 method using L929 cells (L929 China: Typical Cultures Preservation Center). Cells were diluted to a concentration of 1 × 105/mL in a 96-well plate. After 24 h of incubation, the supernatant was discarded, and 100 μL of various film mixtures were added. Following another 24-hour incubation, the supernatant was removed, and 100 μL of CCK8 solution was added. After 2 h, the absorbance was measured at 450 nm. Cell viability of the film samples was calculated using the following Eq:
Cell viability
%
=
OD −
OD 0
OD 1
−
OD 0
× 100 %
where OD, OD1, and OD0 represent the absorbance values at 450 nm for the experimental group (medium containing cells, CCK-8, and the films to be tested), the control group (medium containing cells, CCK-8, without the films), and the blank group (medium without cells or films, CCK-8), respectively [32].

The effectiveness of the composite films for packaging was evaluated using fresh pork, which was procured from a Walmart store in Kunming Province. The selected pork samples (80 g) were uniform in size and color, with no visible signs of damage. The pork was randomly divided into three experimental groups and packaged using different materials (control, PE film, and GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 %). All groups were stored at 4 °C for 10 days. The visual appearance, nutritional quality (including weight loss, pH, firmness, total aerobic bacterial count, TVB-N, and TBARS), and microbial levels of the pork were monitored throughout the storage period according to the previous research [33], which were described in detail in the supplementary materials.
Each group was measured five times in parallel.

Each sample was measured five times. All data were expressed as means ± standard deviation. Significant differences among the data were indicated by different letters and were determined using one-way ANOVA followed by Duncan's multiple comparison test, with significance set at P < 0.05.
The weight-average molecular weight (Mw), monosaccharide composition, FTIR and XRD of SPP were detailly described in the supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidation and antibacterial activity of SPP
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant capacity of SPP was evaluated by DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging assays, and the outcomes are displayed in Fig. 2A. Both DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activities increased significantly with higher concentrations of SPP. The IC50 values for DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging were 1.5 mg/mL and 0.6 mg/mL, respectively. The antioxidant capacities of mushroom polysaccharides are influenced by factors such as molecular weight, degree of sulfation, and the types of glycosidic bonds present [34]. The SPP extracted from Stropharia rugosoannulata primarily consists of glucose, galactose, and glucuronic acid, with glycosyl residues containing both α-type and β-type glycosidic linkages. It has been reported that acidic polysaccharides, predominantly featuring β-type glycosidic linkages in Stropharia rugosoannulata, exhibit stronger antioxidant effects compared to neutral polysaccharides [16,35]. Additionally, Fig. 2B illustrates the growth inhibition rates of E. coli and S. aureus at various concentrations of SPP. The SPP demonstrated substantial antibacterial effects against both bacterial strains, with inhibition rates increasing in correlation with the concentration of SPP. At an SPP concentration of 1.5 mg/mL, the growth of E. coli and S. aureus was inhibited by 69.4 % and 73.3 %, respectively. Morphological alterations in S. aureus and E. coli before and after treatment with SPP were examined using SEM. Fig. 2C and E depict S. aureus and E. coli in the absence of SPP, where the cells exhibited intact morphology with smooth and rounded outer walls. In contrast, post-SPP treatment, the cellular morphology demonstrated pronounced structural disruptions. As illustrated in Fig. 2D and F, the cells appeared wrinkled and shrunken, with some showing signs of cell wall degradation. These observations suggested that SPP might compromise the integrity of the cell wall and membrane in both S. aureus and E. coli, thereby promoting the exocytosis of intracellular proteins and other cellular constituents, ultimately leading to bacterial cell death. Other studies indicated that the antibacterial activity of polysaccharides might be related to their ability to alter bacterial cell membrane permeability, inhibit pathogen adhesion to host cells, and block transmembrane transport of nutrients or energy substances [36,37]. In summary, these findings indicated that SPP possesses significant antioxidant and antimicrobial activities against bacteria.

The TEM image (Fig. 3A) revealed that the SPCDs were nearly spherical particles, uniformly dispersed without any signs of aggregation. High-resolution TEM images further demonstrated that the SPCDs were crystalline, with a lattice spacing of 0.130 nm and a particle size of approximately 2 nm. A distinctive characteristic of carbon dots is their transient color change under UV irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, SPCDs appeared yellow under visible light and emitted a blue hue under UV light. Furthermore, the UV–visible spectrum of the SPCDs solution displayed a prominent absorption peak at around 289 nm, likely attributed to CO or C-OH bonding involved in n-π* transitions, suggesting that SPCDs possessed a strong UV absorption capability, which is crucial for meeting UV-blocking packaging standards [38]. The fluorescence properties of SPCDs were further explored employing fluorescence spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 3B, SPCDs exhibited the strongest fluorescence emission at 468 nm when excited at 347 nm. Additionally, as the excitation wavelength increased from 332 nm to 362 nm, the peak intensity was highest at 347 nm (Fig. 3C), indicating that SPCDs exhibited excitation wavelength dependence.

As shown in Fig. 3D, the XRD patterns of SPCDs exhibited two broad peaks at 7.83° and 23.02° for 2θ values, corresponding to the (002) and (101) diffraction peaks of graphitic carbon. FTIR spectroscopy was employed to identify the chemical functional groups present in SPCDs (Fig. 3E). A broad absorption band at 3414 cm−1 indicated the presence of numerous hydroxyl groups on the surface of the SPCDs. The peak around 2940 cm−1 was linked to the stretching vibration of CH bonds, while the peak at approximately 1640 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CO, consistent with the findings from UV analysis. Additional peaks at 1402, 1023, and 678 cm−1 were associated with the vibrations of COH, COC, and CH (alkyne) groups, respectively [39]. These peaks validated the presence of carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl groups in SPCDs.

Then, the antioxidant capacity of SPCDs was assessed. The measured pH of SPCDs was 5.07, ruling out the influence of extremely low pH on the results. As depicted in Fig. 3F, the scavenging of DPPH and ABTS radicals increased in a concentration-dependent manner, with the high antioxidant capacity attributed to the presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [40]. The antimicrobial activity of SPCDs, evaluated utilizing the inhibition zone method, is depicted in Fig. 3G–H. Specifically, at a concentration of 200 mg/mL, SPCDs exhibited significant antibacterial activity, with inhibition zones measuring 5.2 mm against S. aureus and 4.8 mm against E. coli. In the dark, carbon dots might attach to bacterial cell walls through a combination of diffusion and electrostatic interactions. This attachment led to the sequestration of bacteria, hindering their ability to absorb nutrients and disrupting their physiological metabolism. Carbon dots that accumulated on the bacterial cell membrane/wall can penetrate the delicate membrane, inhibit the formation of bacterial biofilms, and cause cytoplasmic leakage. Once inside the cell, carbon dots can bind to bacterial DNA and RNA, altering their secondary structures and triggering an antibacterial response [41]. Additionally, carbon dots can act as photosensitizers, generating ROS that cause nonspecific damage to bacterial cells upon light exposure, ultimately leading to bacterial death [42]. SPCDs were more effective in inhibiting S. aureus compared to E. coli. The difference in cell wall composition between S. aureus and E. coli is a key factor in determining bacterial resistance to antimicrobial agents. Although the cell wall of E. coli is thinner than that of S. aureus, its more complex structure provides a variety of functional groups on the bacterial surface, contributing to its resistance [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: SEM was used to observe the surface morphology of the films, with the results presented in Fig. 4A–F. The surface of the pure GEL film was smooth and uniform. After the inclusion of SPP, no notable differences were observed on the surface of the GEL/SPP composite film (Fig. 4B). However, as the concentration of SPCDs increased, the presence of carbon dots became visible on the surface of the composite film (Fig. 4C–F). Despite this, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films maintained a relatively homogeneous surface, indicating that SPCDs were well integrated with the biopolymer matrix [44]. Riahi et al. presented SEM images of agar/gelatin films incorporating carbon dots-doped iron-based MOF, which exhibited a comparably dense structure [45].

The crystalline behavior of the composite films is shown in Fig. 4G. All films exhibited a broad diffraction band at approximately 7.5° and 23.5°, corresponding to the triple-helical crystalline structure inherent to gelatin. The diffraction peaks of the GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films did not show any significant changes following the introduction of SPCDs, although the intensity of these peaks at 7.5° and 23.5° was strongest when the SPCDs concentration was 3 wt%. The Xc values of the films initially increased and then decreased with the addition of SPCDs, rising from 57.2 % in the GEL film to 68.3 % in the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 wt% film, before decreasing to 61.7 % in the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-7 wt% film (Fig. 4G). This trend in crystallinity suggested that the 3 wt% SPCD concentration was optimal for promoting the crystalline structure of gelatin. However, when the SPCDs content was increased to 7 wt%, the peak intensities of the GEL/SPP/SPCDs films decreased, indicating that the higher concentration of SPCDs disrupted the crystalline structure of the composite films, possibly due to poor compatibility with the biopolymer matrix [46].

As depicted in Fig. 4H, the pure gelatin film revealed distinctive peaks at 3316 cm−1 and 2923 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of NH in amide A and the asymmetric stretching vibration of CH in amide B, respectively. The bands at approximately 1640 cm−1 and 1556 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of CO bonds in amide I and the bending vibration of NH in amide II, respectively. The peak at 1238 cm−1 corresponded to the in-plane vibration of CN and NH groups in amide III [47]. After the addition of SPP and SPCDs, the FTIR spectra of the composite films remained nearly identical to that of pure GEL, with no new characteristic peaks detected. This lack of new peaks could be attributed to the fact that the amide bond peaks and imine bond peaks (around 1630 and 1550 cm−1) generated by the interaction between the amine groups in GEL with the carbonyl groups on the surface of SPCDs and SPP overlapped with the inherent amide bond peaks of gelatin itself [9]. However, as the SPCD content increased, the intensity of the amide I, II, and III bands gradually intensified, indicating that more interactions (such as imide or amide bonding, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions) occurred between GEL/SPP and SPCDs [48]. Additionally, the characteristic peak of the amide band around 3316 cm−1 shifted to lower wavelengths, suggesting the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between GEL and SPCDs [49].

The mechanical properties of food packaging materials are crucial for ensuring effective protection of food during storage and transportation. Fig. 5A–C illustrate the mechanical characteristics of the various films, including TS, EM, and EB. The pure GEL films exhibited good stretchability, with an EB value of 13.51 ± 0.92 %. The mechanical strength of the GEL/SPP composite films was significantly improved compared to the pure GEL films. As the SPCDs content increased, the TS and EM of the GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films initially increased and then decreased. However, the EB values showed a notable decrease due to the formation of numerous hydrogen bonds between the filler and the biopolymer matrix [50]. Notably, the films containing 3 % SPCDs achieved the highest EM (approximately 3249.54 MPa) and TS (approximately 67.31 MPa) values. These values are comparable to the tensile strength reported for gelatin films enhanced with potato skin carbon dots, as documented by Min et al. [51]. These improvement can be attributed to the uniform dispersion of 3 wt% SPCDs within the biopolymer matrix, resulting in optimal compatibility and abundant hydrogen bonding interactions between the SPCDs and the biopolymer. When a higher concentration of SPCDs was added, aggregation of SPCDs occurred, which hindered the interaction between polymer chains, thereby reducing the TS and EM of the film [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: WVP, WS, and WCA of composite films
ce:section.ce:para: Effective water vapor barrier properties in food packaging films are essential for reducing the rate of water vapor transfer between the food and its environment. Fig. 5D shows the WVP values of different composite films. Both pure GEL films and GEL/SPP composite films exhibited high WVP values, indicating a less effective water vapor barrier. With the addition of SPCDs, the WVP value of the composite film initially decreased and then increased, which could be attributed to the hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of SPCDs. The composite film containing 3 % SPCDs showed the lowest WVP and the best water vapor barrier, likely due to its optimal compatibility, dense structure, and enhanced hydrogen bonding interactions between SPCDs and the GEL/SPP matrix [52]. WS is a critical factor in maintaining the moisture content of products and the dimensional stability of packaging materials. As shown in Fig. 5E–F, pure GEL films exhibited the poorest water resistance, and the WS of the different composite films followed the same trend as WVP. The addition of 5 wt% and 7 wt% SPCDs increased WS, likely due to the excess hydrophilic functional groups on SPCDs that absorbed water after binding to the GEL/SPP matrix. In comparison to pure gelatin films, the WCA values of the GEL/SPP and GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films exhibited some improvement, although no significant differences were noted among them. In summary, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % composite film demonstrated the best water resistance, with WVP, WS, and WCA values of 6.89 × 10−11 g·m/m2·Pa·s, 21.03 %, and 79.41°, respectively. However, compared to the findings of Zhao et al., who researched the film based on gelatin/chitosan cross-linking with anthocyanin-Fe2+ chelate, the composite films developed in this study demonstrated higher water solubility and lower water contact angle [53].

Transparency and UV protection are key functions of food packaging materials, enabling observation of food appearance and protection from UV damage. Fig. 6A–F provide a visual representation of the films. Both pure GEL and GEL/SPP films exhibited high transparency, while the composite films containing SPCDs displayed a slight yellow tint, which became more pronounced as the SPCD content increased. As shown in Table 1 , the brightness (L*) of GEL/SPP films decreased notably with the addition of SPCDs, leading to increases in both reddishness (a*) and yellowishness (b*) and, consequently, a higher ΔE. Despite these changes, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films still maintained relatively high transparency, as evidenced by the visibility of the school badge beneath the films. The transmission spectra of the films were measured from 200 to 800 nm, covering UV-A (320–400 nm), UV-B (275–320 nm), UV-C (200–275 nm), and the visible range (400–760 nm). As shown in Fig. 6G, the transmittance of composite films containing SPCDs was significantly lower compared to GEL and GEL/SPP films, indicating that the inclusion of SPCDs significantly enhanced the UV-blocking properties. This improvement was attributed to the opacity and light-scattering effect of SPCDs, which helped protect packaged foods from UV-induced photo-oxidation and photodegradation [54].

The excellent thermal stability prevents the film from being damaged in a hot environment. TGA and DTG analyses were employed to evaluate the thermal stability of different composite films. As shown in Fig. 7A–B, all the composite films exhibited multi-stage thermal decomposition. The initial phase occurred at approximately 75 °C, corresponding to the release of loosely bound water molecules in the film. With the introduction of SPP, the Tmax1 of the first stage in the GEL/SPP composite film increased to 82.8 °C, surpassing the Tmax1 of the pure GEL film (67.4 °C). Upon incorporation of SPCDs, the Tmax1 value of the composite film further increased to 91.5 °C. The second phase occurred around 258 °C due to the decomposition of SPP, glycerol, and low molecular weight proteins in the composite film. The Tmax2 value of this second stage increased from 257.5 °C (pure GEL film) to 264.7 °C (GEL/SPP film), eventually peaking at 272.4 °C (GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % film). The thermal decomposition of SPP occurred at 284 °C, and the formation of hydrogen bonds between GEL and SPP contributed to the increase in Tmax2 for the GEL/SPP film. The Tmax2 values of the composite films with SPCDs were higher than those of pure GEL and GEL/SPP, demonstrating that the addition of SPCDs enhanced the thermal stability of the composite films. The third phase commenced around 330 °C, corresponding to the breakdown of large protein molecules and SPCDs within the composite film. The Tmax3 value during this stage increased from 328.5 °C (pure GEL film) to 337.4 °C (GEL/SPP film), reaching 340.5 °C (GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % film). The improved thermal stability of the composite films was attributed to the numerous hydrogen bonds formed among GEL, SPP, and SPCDs [55]. Nonetheless, the Tmax3 value of the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-7 % film dropped to 336.4 °C. This was attributed to the relatively poor compatibility of the high concentration of SPCDs with the biopolymer. Additionally, Fig. 7B indicates that the weight loss of GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % was lowest during processing. Overall, the composite films with 3 wt% SPCDs showed the optimal thermal stability.

The antioxidant capacity of food packaging materials is essential, as materials with excellent antioxidant properties can prevent the oxidation of proteins and lipids in food products, thereby preserving food quality. The films were assessed for their ability to scavenge free radical using DPPH and ABTS antioxidant capacity tests. As shown in Fig. 7C, the pure GEL film exhibited the lowest antioxidant capacity, with DPPH radical scavenging at 21.42 % and ABTS radical scavenging at 36.24 %. The addition of SPP to the composite film enhanced its antioxidant properties, due to the abundance of hydroxyl groups in the SPP molecule, which served as effective free radical scavengers with strong antioxidant capacity [56]. The antioxidant capacity of the GEL/SPP/SPCDs films increased significantly with the addition of SPCDs, exhibiting a dose-dependent improvement in antioxidant capacity. This enhancement could be attributed to the presence of multiple electron-donating groups (OH, OCOH) on the surface of SPCDs, which can react directly with free radicals and act as antioxidants [57]. Additionally, the higher antioxidant capacity observed in the ABTS assay compared to the DPPH assay is related to the test solvents used. The hydrophilic behavior of SPP and SPCDs in the composite films allowed for easier release from the aqueous ABTS solution compared to the ethanol solution used in the DPPH assay [58].

Food packaging that can inhibit the reproduction and growth of microorganisms is essential for ensuring food safety and extending shelf life. The colony counting method was applied to evaluate the antimicrobial capacity of different films. The growth inhibition of composite films against E. coli and S. aureus is shown in Fig. 7D. Compared to pure GEL films, the composite films exhibited greater antibacterial effectiveness against both bacteria when SPP was added. The antimicrobial properties of SPP might result from its interaction with sugar receptors in bacteria, causing changes in the cell membrane permeability, generating leakage of bacterial contents and depletion of nutrients [59]. The antimicrobial activity of GEL/SPP/SPCDs films were further enhanced, showing a positive correlation with the SPCDs content. The addition of 7 wt% SPCDs lead to a growth inhibition of 71.14 % against E. coli and 80.42 % against S. aureus in GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films, primarily due to the antimicrobial capability of SPCDs. To assess the ROS generation of SPCDs in films, we investigated the changes in fluorescence signals of HTPA formation and DPBF quenching for OH and O2− generation, respectively. The results are displayed in Fig. 8A–B. The absence of fluorescence in TPA (terephthalic acid) indicates the reaction with photogenerated OHs, causing the solution to fluoresce due to the formation of fluorescent 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTPA). The emission intensities of pure GEL films and GEL/SPP films were minimal, suggesting a lack of OH generation. In contrast, the presence of SPCDs in the films led to an increase in fluorescence intensity, suggesting the production of OH radicals. DPBF, a colorimetric fluorescent probe, acts as a singlet oxygen quencher and undergoes oxidative bleaching when exposed to O2− radicals, resulting in the formation of colorless 1,2-dibenzoylbenzene [60]. Both pure GEL films and GEL/SPP films exhibited the most intense and consistent emission, indicating the absence of O2− production. Films containing SPCDs experienced a notable reduction in DPBF emission intensity at 485 nm, with the decline in emission spectra intensity increasing as the SPCDs content rose, indicating a gradual increase in O2− radical generation [61]. The dependence of effective radical generation on SPCDs was confirmed. Moreover, the antibacterial activity of the composite films against S. aureus was superior to that of E. coli, which was consistent to the antibacterial efficacy of SPCDs alone. Similar findings were reported by Wang et al., who investigated the Chitosan/dialdehyde carboxymethyl cellulose composite films incorporated with zein-loaded cinnamaldehyde nanoparticles to fabricate the active food packaging materials [26]. In conclusion, these results indicated that GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films had good antibacterial activity.

CCK8 assays were conducted to evaluate the in vitro cytotoxicity effects of the different composite films on the L929 mouse fibroblast cell line, with the findings depicted in Fig. 8C. Cell viability was >80 % for all GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films, indicating that they were safe for biological applications [62]. In addition, previous research on the cytotoxicity of carbon dots has demonstrated that concentrations of 10–100 μg/mL generally resulted in only a minor decrease in cell viability (around 10–20 %) in most cases. This level of toxicity was considered acceptable for applications such as cell labeling and staining, suggesting that these carbon dots can be safely used in food packaging [63]. These findings indicated that GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films are eco-friendly materials with minimal toxicity, making them suitable for use in food packaging.

Pork is a rich source of essential amino acids, iron, and vitamins, and plays a crucial role in the production and maintenance of red blood cells in humans [64]. However, pork is highly susceptible to microbial growth and multiplication, lipid oxidation, and spoilage [65]. In this study, we selected GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % composite films, which exhibited the best functional properties, for preserving fresh pork at 4 °C.

The alterations in the visual characteristics of pork during storage are shown in Fig. 9A. From day 4 onwards, the pork in the control and PE groups began to deteriorate and darken significantly, likely due to microbial infection. However, the appearance of the pork in the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % group remained unchanged until day 10, indicating a superior preservation effect. The pork in the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % group displayed a yellowish color due to the yellowish tint of composite film.

Pork has a high respiration rate and is prone to rapid weight loss [66], which reduces its freshness. The weight loss rates of pork in the different groups are presented in Fig. 9B. The control group of pork experienced the highest weight loss rate, as no protective measures were in place, allowing water to escape easily into the surrounding environment. In contrast, the PE group, exhibited the lowest weight loss rate, attributed to the superior water vapor resistance of the PE film. The weight loss rate in the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % group was lower than that in the control group, which was related to the water vapor resistance of the composite film.

As shown in Fig. 9C, the pH value of pork stored at 4 °C initially decreased and then increased, reflecting the two phases pork undergoes post-slaughter: rigor mortis and spoilage. During rigor mortis, muscle glycogen in pork undergoes anaerobic glycolysis, producing lactic acid, while adenosine triphosphate catabolism generates phosphoric acid, both contributing to a lower pH [67]. In the spoilage phase, bacterial activity and endogenous enzymes break down proteins, leading to the formation of alkaline nitrogenous compounds like ammonia and amines, which cause an increase in pH levels. Compared to the control and PE groups, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % group exhibited the smallest pH increase, indicating superior preservation of pork.

As shown in Fig. 9D, pork firmness gradually decreased in all groups throughout the storage period, which could be attributed to the reduction of cellular compressive strength, protein denaturation, and membrane structure disruption [68]. Better firmness was observed in PE and GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % groups, which indicated that the composite films were effective in reducing the disruption of cellular membranes by microbial growth.

As shown in Fig. 9E, the total bacterial counts in all pork samples increased gradually over the storage period. In the control and PE groups, bacterial counts surpassed 6.0 log CFU/g threshold, reaching 7.60 log CFU/g and 8.52 log CFU/g, respectively, by the end of the storage period, which were higher than those in the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % group. The PE group had the highest bacteria counts, likely due to water vapor from pork respiration condensing on the pork surface and inside of the PE film, creating a humid environment conducive to bacterial growth [69]. By the conclusion of the storage period, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % group had a total aerobic bacterial count of the 5.21 log CFU/g, a reduction of 2.39 log CFU/g compared to the control group.

Microbial decay and biochemical reactions are the primary factors contributing to the deterioration of pork during storage. Protein in pork is degraded by enzymes and bacteria into harmful small molecules such as histamine and tyramine [70]. Total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) is comprised of these substances and other simple nitrogenous compounds during storage. According to the Chinese National Standard GB 2707–2016, the maximum allowable TVB-N level in fresh pork is 15 mg/100 g [71]. On the 6th day, the TVB-N level in pork from control group exceeded the national threshold (Fig. 9F), while the level in pork from the PE group also exceeded the limit on the 8th day, indicating that the pork had spoiled. In contrast, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % group maintained TVB-N levels below the national limit throughout the entire storage period. Compared to the apple pectin/chitosan-based active films integrated with apple polyphenol/hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin inclusion complex, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % composite film developed in this study demonstrated lower TVB-N levels and superior pork preservation capabilities [72].

The level of Thio barbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) in meat products is a critical indicator of secondary oxidation products associated with oxidative rancidity and plays a significant role in determining shelf-life [73]. Fig. 9G illustrates the fluctuations in TBARS levels among various experimental groups over the course of storage. Over the 10-day storage period, TBARS levels steadily increased in all groups, with the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % group exhibiting significantly lower levels compared to the control and PE groups. This finding indicated that the composite film effectively inhibited lipid oxidation. In conclusion, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % film successfully slowed the deterioration of pork quality and extended the storage time of pork at 4 °C for up to 10 days.

In this study, gelatin (GEL) was used as a matrix and Stropharia rugosoannulata polysaccharides (SPP) were added to form GEL/SPP composite films. Stropharia rugosoannulata carbon dots (SPCDs) were synthesized by a hydrothermal method and used as active fillers in the GEL/SPP films. Various SPCDs contents composite films were thoroughly analyzed for their morphological, mechanical, optical, thermal, and functional characteristics. The findings demonstrated that the GEL/SPP/SPCDs composite films delivered outstanding mechanical strengths, effective UV and moisture barriers, and robust thermal stability. The GEL/SPP/SPCDs films also showed significant antioxidant effectiveness against DPPH and ABTS radicals and strong antimicrobial activities. Specifically, the GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % films, possessing comprehensive physicochemical and functional properties, were applied for pork preservation. The results indicated that the composite film was proved to successfully enhance the pork's shelf life at 4 °C for up to 10 days by mitigating lipid oxidation and suppressing microbial proliferation. Building upon the findings of WS and WCA, the composite films were constrained by their comparatively low water contact angle and moderately high water solubility, indicating potential directions for future research. In conclusion, the synthesized GEL/SPP/SPCDs-3 % films exhibit considerable potential as candidates for active food packaging applications.

Jintao Wu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Zhaolan Yan: Validation, Methodology. Peng Shan: Software, Data curation. Wei Li: Data curation, Conceptualization. Kun Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Conceptualization. Hui Li: Supervision, Software, Conceptualization.
